refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and enable continued operation under communication loss.
DP6: Establish comprehensive health monitoring and diagnostic system with automated alerting and decision logic to enable rapid human intervention when autonomous recovery fails.
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular design with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces, and (3) comprehensive health monitoring with automated diagnostics and human-in-the-loop recovery protocols, designed to achieve target availability through layered fault tolerance and rapid restoration.

potential_sources: Redundancy and modularity are largely orthogonal; however, redundant subsystems must share standardized interfaces (DP2 constraint) to avoid coupling through custom integration logic.
Health monitoring (DP6) is tightly coupled to failover logic (DP1) — both must operate on consistent state definitions and timing assumptions; decoupling requires explicit event contracts.
Graceful degradation (DP4) and redundancy (DP1) can conflict if degradation rules are not clearly prioritized; requires explicit mode-switching logic to avoid oscillation.

Distributed control (DP5) and centralized monitoring (DP6) create a potential coupling through diagnostic data flow; mitigate by defining asynchronous, event-driven interfaces rather than synchronous polling.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is a composition of complementary strategies. Coupling is manageable if each sub-strategy (redundancy, modularity, monitoring, degradation, distribution) is realized with explicit interface contracts and clear ownership boundaries. The main risk is architectural diffusion: if redundancy, modularity, and monitoring are not co-designed as a coherent system, they will create hidden dependencies and increase complexity.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Target availability of ≥0.97 is achievable with the proposed architecture given the worksite environment (e.g., dust, vibration, temperature extremes). Risk: Environmental stressors may degrade component reliability faster than predicted; mitigation requires environmental hardening and accelerated testing.

ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and schedule constraints. Risk: Redundancy increases BOM, weight, and complexity; customer may reject if cost/benefit is unfavorable. Mitigation: Conduct trade study on redundancy scope (full vs. partial).

ASSUMPTION: Hot-swappable components can be designed without compromising safety or performance. Risk: Mechanical/electrical interfaces for hot-swap may introduce new failure modes (e.g., contact wear, misalignment). Mitigation: Rigorous design review and field validation.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance data (condition monitoring) is available and reliable. Risk: Sensors may fail, data may be noisy, or degradation models may be inaccurate in field conditions. Mitigation: Implement sensor redundancy and conservative thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Human operators are available and trained to respond to automated alerts within acceptable RTO. Risk: Operator unavailability or slow response degrades effective availability. Mitigation: Define clear escalation procedures and consider autonomous recovery for common failure modes.

ASSUMPTION: Distributed control can be implemented without introducing new failure modes (e.g., network partitions, clock skew, deadlock). Risk: Distributed systems are inherently complex; testing and validation are challenging. Mitigation: Formal verification of critical coordination logic.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is representative of long-term operational conditions. Risk: Short evaluation window may not capture rare failure modes or seasonal variations. Mitigation: Extend evaluation period or use accelerated testing to validate availability claims.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold (≥0.97 or as specified) over the agreed evaluation period, through a combination of (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular architecture enabling rapid component replacement, (3) predictive and condition-based maintenance, and (4) comprehensive health monitoring with human-in-the-loop recovery. The solution shall define explicit recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for each failure mode, and shall be validated under representative worksite conditions (environmental stressors, duty cycles, maintenance intervals) to ensure availability claims are achievable in practice. Acceptance criteria: Measured availability ≥0.97 over evaluation period, with documented evidence of redundancy effectiveness, mean time to recovery (MTTR) ≤ [customer-defined threshold], and zero safety-critical failures.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized health monitoring, fault detection, automated diagnostics, failover coordination, and human-in-the-loop recovery protocols for the integrated availability architecture

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives health status, subsystem state, and performance metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation for continuous monitoring and diagnostics

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits fault notifications, failover triggers, and recovery directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission context and sends recovery/failover status updates to NGHE Solution for human-in-the-loop decision support

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with remote monitoring and human operators for diagnostics validation and recovery protocol approval

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: health monitoring, diagnostics, and human-in-the-loop recovery are core safety functions. Redundancy coordination and failover logic naturally belong here. Minor diffusion: physical redundancy implementation (hot-swappable components, standardized interfaces) must be realized in Vehicle module, but Safety Services owns the orchestration logic.

Moderate fit: contains physical redundancy, hot-swappable components, and standardized interfaces. However, health monitoring logic, diagnostics algorithms, and human-in-the-loop protocols would diffuse significantly to Safety Services and NGHE Solution. Ownership split creates coupling and reduces cohesion.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is operator-facing and mission-execution focused. Availability architecture (redundancy, failover, diagnostics) is orthogonal to teleoperation control. Significant diffusion of monitoring and recovery logic to Safety Services and Vehicle.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level orchestrator. While it may coordinate human-in-the-loop decisions, the detailed health monitoring, automated diagnostics, and failover mechanisms belong at a lower architectural level. Containment here would create excessive coupling and reduce modularity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of DP7 because availability, fault tolerance, and recovery are safety-critical concerns. The module already owns health monitoring and diagnostics responsibilities. By centralizing redundancy coordination, failover logic, and human-in-the-loop protocols in Safety Services, we achieve high cohesion (all availability mechanisms grouped together) and low coupling (Vehicle implements physical redundancy and interfaces; Safety Services orchestrates via SafetyAlert and Telemetry; NGHE Solution provides human oversight via Tasking and Network Link).
containment_risks: Risk: Physical redundancy implementation (hot-swappable components, standardized interfaces) resides in Vehicle module. Safety Services must interface cleanly via SafetyAlert and Telemetry to avoid tight coupling.

Risk: Human-in-the-loop recovery protocols require coordination with NGHE Solution. Ensure Tasking interface clearly defines decision boundaries (Safety Services proposes, NGHE Solution approves/executes).
Risk: Automated failover logic must not conflict with Teleoperation active control. Define clear handoff semantics via SafetyAlert to prevent race conditions.
Risk: Network Link dependency for remote diagnostics introduces external latency. Ensure local failover mechanisms in Safety Services can operate autonomously if network is degraded.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses the FR by specifying a three-pillar integrated availability architecture: (1) redundancy with automatic failover, (2) modular design with hot-swappable components, and (3) comprehensive health monitoring with automated diagnostics and human-in-the-loop recovery. These mechanisms are logically sound for achieving and maintaining availability ≥0.97. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide design without over-constraining implementation. No logical gaps detected between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Physical redundancy implementation (hot-swappable components, standardized interfaces) is distributed across Vehicle module while orchestration logic resides in Safety Services. This DP<->IM bridge requires clean interface contracts via SafetyAlert and Telemetry to prevent tight coupling and ensure Safety Services can coordinate failover without deep knowledge of Vehicle internals.

Human-in-the-loop recovery protocols span Safety Services (proposal/diagnostics) and NGHE Solution (approval/execution). Decision boundary ambiguity on Tasking interface could lead to race conditions or conflicting directives. Recommend explicit state machine defining who owns each recovery phase.

Automated failover logic in Safety Services must coexist with active Teleoperation control. Handoff semantics via SafetyAlert are critical; undefined priority or timing could cause oscillation or loss of operator authority.

Network Link dependency for remote diagnostics and operator approval introduces external latency. If network degrades, Safety Services must operate autonomously with pre-approved recovery policies. Risk of inconsistent behavior if local vs. remote decision logic diverges.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal owner with containment_score 0.92. Health monitoring, diagnostics, failover coordination, and human-in-the-loop protocols are cohesively grouped. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and well-understood: Vehicle owns physical redundancy realization; Safety Services owns orchestration. Ownership split is clean and reduces coupling. Vehicle (0.65 containment) and NGHE Solution (0.5 containment) are secondary contributors with clear, bounded responsibilities. Teleoperation (0.45 containment) correctly excluded from availability architecture ownership, avoiding orthogonal coupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is directly verifiable against acceptance criterion (≥0.97 over agreed evaluation period). Safety Services module can expose availability metrics via Telemetry interface and log failover events for post-evaluation audit. Automated diagnostics and recovery actions are traceable via SafetyAlert and Tasking logs. Human-in-the-loop decisions are recorded on Network Link. Feasibility is high provided evaluation period is defined and measurement instrumentation is in place.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns health monitoring, diagnostics, failover coordination, and human-in-the-loop recovery logic. Vehicle owns physical redundancy and hot-swappable component interfaces. NGHE Solution owns mission-level human oversight and recovery approval. Teleoperation owns operator control during normal operation. Interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are named and directional. Ownership candidate is unambiguous. Recommended clarification: define explicit state machine for human-in-the-loop recovery phases (detect → diagnose → propose → approve → execute → restore) to eliminate decision boundary ambiguity.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the human-in-the-loop recovery state machine on the Tasking interface: define which module owns each phase (Safety Services proposes, NGHE Solution approves, Safety Services executes, Vehicle restores). Include timeout and escalation rules.

Define failover priority and handoff semantics between Safety Services automated failover and active Teleoperation control. Specify: (a) conditions under which Safety Services can override Teleoperation, (b) notification latency requirements, (c) operator ability to veto or revert failover.

Specify autonomous failover policy for Safety Services when Network Link is degraded or unavailable. Document pre-approved recovery actions that do not require remote operator approval, and conditions for re-synchronization when network restores.

Add acceptance criterion for failover latency (e.g., 'failover shall complete within X seconds of fault detection') to ensure availability target is achievable. Current criterion (≥0.97) does not constrain recovery time.

Document interface contracts for SafetyAlert (failover triggers, directives, acknowledgments) and Telemetry (health status schema, diagnostic data format) to ensure Vehicle and Safety Services implementations remain decoupled and testable.

Clarify scope of 'standardized interfaces' for hot-swappable components: specify which standards (e.g., CAN, Ethernet, mechanical connectors) and which subsystems are hot-swappable. This reduces ambiguity in Vehicle module realization.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR↔DP, DP↔IM, FR↔IM) pass with high confidence. The three-pillar availability architecture is logically sound, ownership is clear (Safety Services as primary orchestrator with well-bounded diffusion to Vehicle and NGHE Solution), and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical design details that must be resolved before detailed implementation: (1) human-in-the-loop recovery state machine is ambiguous, (2) failover handoff semantics with active Teleoperation are undefined, (3) autonomous failover policy for network degradation is missing, (4) failover latency acceptance criterion is absent, and (5) interface contracts are underspecified. These are not logical gaps in the DP itself, but rather necessary refinements to guide the next decomposition level and reduce implementation risk. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-FR derivation now—while capturing these revisions as constraints—is justified. The sub-FRs will naturally decompose the three pillars (redundancy, modularity, monitoring) into more granular requirements that will force resolution of these ambiguities.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Detect component faults and system degradation in real-time with latency ≤ T_detect (to be specified, e.g., ≤500ms) to enable timely failover decision.
FR7.2: Diagnose root cause of detected faults and assess recovery feasibility (autonomous vs. human-approved) within latency ≤ T_diagnose (to be specified, e.g., ≤2s).
FR7.3: Execute automated failover to redundant component or safe state within latency ≤ T_failover (to be specified, e.g., ≤5s) when fault is detected and autonomous recovery is pre-approved.
FR7.4: Coordinate human-in-the-loop recovery approval via NGHE Solution when fault requires operator decision, with decision timeout and escalation rules defined.
FR7.5: Manage handoff between Safety Services automated failover and active Teleoperation control to prevent oscillation, loss of operator authority, or conflicting directives.
FR7.6: Operate autonomously with pre-approved recovery policies when Network Link is degraded or unavailable, and re-synchronize state when network restores.
FR7.7: Maintain physical redundancy and hot-swappable component interfaces (standardized connectors, mechanical/electrical specs) to enable modular replacement without system shutdown.
FR7.8: Expose availability metrics, failover events, and diagnostic logs via Telemetry interface for real-time monitoring and post-evaluation audit.

revise.why: DP7 is logically sound but must be refined to incorporate the six recommended_revisions before sub-DP derivation. Specifically: (1) formalize human-in-the-loop recovery state machine with explicit phase ownership and timeout/escalation rules; (2) define failover priority and handoff semantics with Teleoperation (conditions for override, notification latency, operator veto capability); (3) specify autonomous failover policy for network degradation with pre-approved actions and re-sync conditions; (4) add failover latency acceptance criterion (e.g., ≤5s) to constrain recovery time and ensure ≥0.97 availability is achievable; (5) document interface contracts for SafetyAlert and Telemetry to ensure Vehicle and Safety Services remain decoupled; (6) clarify scope of 'standardized interfaces' for hot-swappable components (which standards, which subsystems). These refinements will eliminate ambiguity in the DP and provide clear constraints for sub-DP derivation.

assumptions: Evaluation period for availability measurement is defined (e.g., 1000 operating hours) and measurement instrumentation is in place.
Network Link is available for remote diagnostics and operator approval in nominal operation; degradation is a fault condition, not nominal.
Teleoperation is active during normal operation; Safety Services failover does not preempt operator control except in safety-critical scenarios (to be defined).
NGHE Solution has authority to approve or reject recovery proposals from Safety Services; approval latency is acceptable (e.g., ≤30s for non-critical faults).
Vehicle module can expose health status and accept failover directives via SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces without tight coupling to Safety Services logic.
Hot-swappable components are physically and electrically isolated such that replacement does not require system shutdown or manual reconfiguration.
Automated diagnostics can distinguish between transient faults (retry) and permanent faults (failover) with acceptable false-positive rate (to be specified).
open_risks: Human-in-the-loop recovery decision boundary is ambiguous: unclear which module owns each phase (detect, diagnose, propose, approve, execute, restore). Risk of race conditions or conflicting directives if Safety Services and NGHE Solution act simultaneously.

Failover handoff semantics with active Teleoperation are undefined: no specification of conditions under which Safety Services can override operator control, notification latency, or operator ability to veto failover. Risk of oscillation or loss of operator authority.

Autonomous failover policy for network degradation is missing: no specification of pre-approved recovery actions or conditions for re-synchronization. Risk of inconsistent behavior if local vs. remote decision logic diverges.
Failover latency acceptance criterion is absent: current FR7 specifies availability ≥0.97 but does not constrain recovery time. Risk that failover latency is too long to achieve target availability.

Interface contracts for SafetyAlert and Telemetry are underspecified: no formal schema for failover triggers, directives, acknowledgments, health status, or diagnostic data. Risk of implementation divergence and integration failures.

Scope of 'standardized interfaces' for hot-swappable components is unclear: no specification of which standards (CAN, Ethernet, mechanical connectors) or which subsystems are hot-swappable. Risk of ambiguity in Vehicle module realization.
Network Link dependency for remote diagnostics and operator approval introduces external latency and single point of failure. Risk of unavailability if network is degraded or partitioned.
evidence_needed: Formal state machine diagram for human-in-the-loop recovery phases with explicit ownership, timeout rules, and escalation paths.
Failover priority matrix and handoff protocol defining conditions under which Safety Services can override Teleoperation, notification latency, and operator veto capability.
Autonomous failover policy document specifying pre-approved recovery actions for network degradation, decision criteria, and re-sync conditions.
Failover latency acceptance criterion (e.g., ≤5s from fault detection to failover completion) derived from availability target ≥0.97 and expected fault frequency.
Interface contract specifications for SafetyAlert (failover triggers, directives, acknowledgments, error codes) and Telemetry (health status schema, diagnostic data format, update frequency).
Standardized interface specification for hot-swappable components (mechanical, electrical, CAN/Ethernet protocols, connector types, subsystem scope).
Network Link availability and latency requirements (e.g., 99.9% availability, ≤500ms round-trip latency) to support remote diagnostics and operator approval.
Evaluation plan defining measurement period, availability calculation method (e.g., MTBF/MTTR), and instrumentation for failover event logging and audit.
